content_en:
One of Ibn Hajar's most important contributions in Al-Isabah is his precise formulation of the definition of a companion. Earlier scholars had used various definitions, and the differences between them had practical consequences for which individuals qualified. Ibn Hajar's definition became the standard: a companion is anyone who met the Prophet while believing in him and died in the state of Islam.

This definition has several components, each carefully chosen. The meeting must have been while the person was a Muslim — meeting the Prophet before conversion or after conversion but before belief was genuine does not qualify. The meeting could be in person (which is the standard case) or, in unusual circumstances, through other forms of direct contact. The person must have died in Islam — apostates who subsequently left the faith do not qualify as companions in the theological sense, even if they had genuine contact with the Prophet.

Ibn Hajar also clarifies that the definition does not require extended contact or transmission of hadith. A person who met the Prophet once, even briefly, and died as a Muslim is a companion. This broad definition includes many individuals who are known by little more than their name and the fact of their meeting with the Prophet. These obscure companions are nonetheless listed in Al-Isabah, even in brief entries, because their status as companions is theologically significant.

The definition excludes figures who claimed companion status on the basis of conversion after the Prophet's death (some early converts to Islam who never personally met the Prophet were sometimes counted as companions by loose definition), and it excludes those who met the Prophet only in vision or dream — a category of claim that Ibn Hajar addresses and firmly rejects as a basis for companion status.

Applying this definition to thousands of cases required exactly the kind of rigorous evidential analysis that Ibn Hajar was uniquely equipped to provide. His ability to evaluate the chains of transmission through which claims about specific individuals reached him, and his encyclopedic knowledge of the broader biographical literature, made his judgments authoritative in ways that earlier scholars' could not always be.
content_id:
Salah satu kontribusi terpenting Ibnu Hajar dalam Al-Isabah adalah formulasi tepatnya tentang definisi sahabat. Para ulama sebelumnya telah menggunakan berbagai definisi, dan perbedaan di antara mereka memiliki konsekuensi praktis bagi siapa yang memenuhi syarat. Definisi Ibnu Hajar menjadi standar: sahabat adalah siapa pun yang bertemu dengan Nabi ﷺ dalam keadaan beriman kepada beliau dan meninggal dalam keadaan Islam.

Definisi ini memiliki beberapa komponen, masing-masing dipilih dengan hati-hati. Pertemuan harus terjadi saat orang tersebut sudah Muslim — bertemu Nabi sebelum masuk Islam atau setelah masuk Islam tetapi sebelum keimanan sungguh-sungguh tidak memenuhi syarat. Pertemuan bisa secara langsung (yang merupakan kasus standar) atau, dalam keadaan yang tidak biasa, melalui bentuk kontak langsung lainnya. Orang tersebut harus meninggal dalam Islam — orang-orang murtad yang kemudian meninggalkan iman tidak memenuhi syarat sebagai sahabat dalam pengertian teologis.

Ibnu Hajar juga memperjelas bahwa definisi ini tidak mensyaratkan kontak yang lama atau periwayatan hadis. Seseorang yang bertemu Nabi ﷺ sekali saja, bahkan sebentar, dan meninggal sebagai Muslim adalah sahabat. Definisi luas ini mencakup banyak individu yang hanya diketahui nama dan kenyataan pertemuan mereka dengan Nabi. Para sahabat yang tidak dikenal ini tetap terdaftar dalam Al-Isabah, meskipun dalam entri yang singkat, karena status mereka sebagai sahabat penting secara teologis.

Menerapkan definisi ini pada ribuan kasus memerlukan analisis bukti yang ketat seperti yang Ibnu Hajar adalah ahlinya. Kemampuannya dalam mengevaluasi sanad-sanad yang melaluinya klaim-klaim tentang individu tertentu sampai kepadanya, dan pengetahuan ensiklopedisnya tentang literatur biografi yang lebih luas, membuat penilaiannya otoritatif dengan cara yang tidak selalu dapat dilakukan oleh ulama-ulama sebelumnya.
content_ar: One of Ibn Hajar's most المهم contributions in Al-Isabah is his precise formulation of the definition of a companion. Earlier العلماء had used various definitions, and the differences between them had practical consequences for which individuals qualified. Ibn Hajar's definition became the standard: a companion is anyone who met النبي ﷺ بينما believing in him and died in the state of الإسلام. This definition has several components, each carefully chosen. The meeting must have been بينما the person was a مسلم — meeting النبي ﷺ قبل conversion or بعد conversion but قبل الاعتقاد was genuine does not qualify. The meeting could be in person (which is the standard case) or, in unusual circumstances, through other forms of direct contact. The person must have died in الإسلام — apostates who subsequently left the الإيمان do not qualify as الصحابة in the theological sense, even إذا they had genuine contact with النبي ﷺ. Ibn Hajar أيضاً clarifies that the definition does not require extended contact or transmission of الحديث. A person who met النبي ﷺ حين, even briefly, and died as a مسلم is a companion. This broad definition includes many individuals who are known by little more than their name and the fact of their meeting with النبي ﷺ. These obscure الصحابة are nonetheless listed in Al-Isabah, even in brief entries, لأن their status as الصحابة is theologically المهم. The definition excludes figures who claimed companion status on the basis of conversion بعد النبي ﷺ's الموت (some early converts to الإسلام who never personally met النبي ﷺ were sometimes counted as الصحابة by loose definition), and it excludes those who met النبي ﷺ only in vision or dream — a category of claim that Ibn Hajar addresses and firmly rejects as a basis for companion status. Applying this definition to thousands of cases required exactly the kind of rigorous evidential analysis that Ibn Hajar was uniquely equipped to provide. His ability to evaluate the chains of transmission through which claims about specific individuals reached him, and his encyclopedic العلم of the broader biographical literature, made his judgments المعتمد in ways that earlier العلماء' could not always be.
